content.body:
Mole concept:
1 mole = 6.022×10²³ particles (Avogadro's number, Nₐ)

n = m/M  (moles = mass/molar mass)
n = V/22.4  at STP  (for gases)
n = cV  (for solutions, c in mol/dm³)

Molar mass = sum of atomic masses of all atoms in formula
H₂O: 2(1)+16 = 18 g/mol
NaCl: 23+35.5 = 58.5 g/mol
H₂SO₄: 2+32+4(16) = 98 g/mol

content.title: Mole Calculations
content.body:
Mass of 2 mol CaCO₃ (100 g/mol):
mass = n × M = 2 × 100 = 200 g

Moles in 9g of H₂O (18 g/mol):
n = 9/18 = 0.5 mol

Particles in 0.25 mol:
N = 0.25 × 6.022×10²³ = 1.5×10²³ particles

content.title: Empirical and Molecular Formulae
content.body:
Empirical formula: simplest whole number ratio of atoms
Molecular formula: actual number of atoms in molecule

Find empirical formula of compound: 40%C, 6.7%H, 53.3%O
C: 40/12=3.33, H: 6.7/1=6.7, O: 53.3/16=3.33
Ratio C:H:O = 1:2:1 → empirical formula: CH₂O
If molar mass=180: CH₂O×6 → C₆H₁₂O₆ (glucose)

questions.question: Avogadro's number (6.022×10²³) is the number of particles in
questions.explanation: One mole of any substance contains 6.022×10²³ particles (atoms, molecules, or ions).

questions.question: Molar mass of H₂SO₄ (H=1, S=32, O=16)
Moles in 44g of CO₂ (44 g/mol)
Mass of 3 mol of NaOH (40 g/mol)
Number of molecules in 0.5 mol of CO₂
Volume of 2 mol of gas at STP
Empirical formula is
questions.explanation: Empirical formula gives the simplest integer ratio of elements, not necessarily the actual molecular formula.

questions.question: Compound: 75% C, 25% H. Empirical formula (C=12,H=1)
Moles of solute in 250cm³ of 0.4 mol/dm³ solution
Molar mass is measured in
questions.explanation: Molar mass is grams per mole (g/mol) — the mass of one mole of a substance.

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body: Stoichiometry uses the mole ratios in balanced equations to calculate masses, volumes, and concentrations of reactants and products.

Steps for stoichiometry calculations:
1. Write balanced equation
2. Convert given quantity to moles
3. Use mole ratio from equation
4. Convert moles to required quantity

Mole ratio = coefficients in balanced equation
2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O
2 mol H₂ : 1 mol O₂ : 2 mol H₂O

content.title: Mass Calculations
content.body:
How much CaO from 50g CaCO₃?
CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂
MM: CaCO₃=100, CaO=56
n(CaCO₃) = 50/100 = 0.5 mol
n(CaO) = 0.5 mol (1:1 ratio)
mass(CaO) = 0.5 × 56 = 28g

content.title: Limiting Reagent and Yield
content.body:
Limiting reagent: reactant that runs out first (limits product)
Excess reagent: reactant left over

Theoretical yield: maximum mass of product (from stoichiometry)
Actual yield: what you actually get in experiment
% yield = (actual yield/theoretical yield) × 100
% atom economy = (molar mass of desired product / total molar mass of all products) × 100

questions.question: In 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O, the mole ratio H₂:O₂:H₂O is
questions.explanation: Coefficients give the mole ratio directly: 2 mol H₂ : 1 mol O₂ : 2 mol H₂O

questions.question: Moles of O₂ needed to react with 4 mol H₂ (2H₂+O₂→2H₂O)
questions.explanation: Ratio H₂:O₂ = 2:1, so 4 mol H₂ needs 4/2 = 2 mol O₂

questions.question: Mass of CO₂ from burning 12g carbon (C+O₂→CO₂, C=12,CO₂=44)
questions.explanation: n(C)=12/12=1mol. 1mol C→1mol CO₂. mass(CO₂)=44g

questions.question: Limiting reagent is
options: reactant completely consumed that limits product amount
questions.answer: reactant completely consumed that limits product amount
questions.explanation: The limiting reagent is fully used up first, determining the maximum amount of product that can form.

questions.question: % yield =
questions.explanation: % yield = (actual yield obtained / theoretical maximum yield) × 100

questions.question: Theoretical yield of 100% would mean
options: all possible product is obtained with no losses
questions.answer: all possible product is obtained with no losses
questions.explanation: 100% yield = perfectly efficient reaction with no side reactions, incomplete reaction, or product loss.

questions.question: Atom economy = (molar mass of desired product / total molar mass of products) × 100. A reaction making only one product has atom economy of
questions.explanation: If the only product is the desired product, all atoms end up in useful product → 100% atom economy.

questions.question: 3g H₂ reacts with excess N₂ (N₂+3H₂→2NH₃). Moles of NH₃ formed (H=1, N=14)
questions.explanation: n(H₂)=3/2=1.5mol. Ratio H₂:NH₃=3:2, so n(NH₃)=1.5×(2/3)=1mol... wait: n(H₂)=1.5, × (2/3) = 1 mol NH₃

questions.question: Why might actual yield be less than theoretical yield?
options: Reaction incomplete, side reactions, or product lost during collection
questions.answer: Reaction incomplete, side reactions, or product lost during collection
questions.explanation: Real reactions may not go to completion, produce unwanted byproducts, or lose product during filtration/transfer.

questions.question: Percentage purity = (mass of pure substance / total mass of sample) × 100. A 5g sample containing 4g NaCl has purity of
What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body: Titration is a precise technique to find the concentration of an unknown solution by reacting it with a standard solution of known concentration.

Titration steps:
1. Fill burette with standard solution (titrant)
2. Pipette exact volume of unknown into conical flask
3. Add indicator
4. Add titrant until endpoint (colour change)
5. Record volume used (titre)
6. Repeat for concordant results (within 0.1cm³)

Calculation:
n = cV → find moles of known
Use mole ratio → moles of unknown
c = n/V → concentration of unknown

content.title: Acid-Base Titration
content.body:
NaOH + HCl → NaCl + H₂O
25cm³ of NaOH of unknown concentration
Titrated with 0.1 mol/dm³ HCl
Volume HCl used: 20cm³

n(HCl) = 0.1 × 0.020 = 0.002 mol
Ratio 1:1, so n(NaOH) = 0.002 mol
c(NaOH) = 0.002/0.025 = 0.08 mol/dm³

content.title: Indicators
content.body:
Litmus: red (acid) ↔ purple (neutral) ↔ blue (alkali)
Phenolphthalein: colourless (acid) → pink/red (alkali)
Methyl orange: red (acid) → yellow (alkali)

Suitable for strong acid/strong base: either indicator
Weak acid/strong base: phenolphthalein
Strong acid/weak base: methyl orange

questions.question: In a titration, the burette contains the
questions.explanation: The burette contains the titrant — the solution of known concentration added to the flask.

questions.question: Concordant results in titration means
options: two or more readings within 0.1cm³ of each other
questions.answer: two or more readings within 0.1cm³ of each other
questions.explanation: Concordant results: repeated titres that agree closely (within 0.1cm³), confirming precision.

questions.question: In NaOH+HCl→NaCl+H₂O, 20cm³ of 0.2M HCl neutralises NaOH. Moles of HCl =
Using result above (1:1 ratio), if NaOH volume was 25cm³, concentration =
Phenolphthalein is colourless in acid and turns pink in alkali. It is best for
questions.explanation: Phenolphthalein endpoint is in the alkaline range — ideal when the equivalence point is slightly alkaline (weak acid/strong base).

questions.question: The endpoint in acid-base titration is when
questions.explanation: Endpoint: the point at which the indicator changes colour permanently, indicating neutralisation is complete.

questions.question: A back titration is used when
options: the analyte is insoluble or reacts too slowly for direct titration
questions.answer: the analyte is insoluble or reacts too slowly for direct titration
questions.explanation: Back titration: add excess known reagent, then titrate the excess with another standard solution. Used for insoluble/slow-reacting substances.

questions.question: Why must the pipette be rinsed with the solution it will measure?
options: To prevent dilution of the measured solution
questions.answer: To prevent dilution of the measured solution
questions.explanation: Rinsing with the sample solution removes any water that would otherwise dilute the solution and give incorrect results.

questions.question: Methyl orange is red in acid and yellow in alkali. It is best for
questions.explanation: Methyl orange endpoint is acidic — ideal when equivalence point is slightly acidic (strong acid/weak base).

questions.question: What is the purpose of a rough titration?
options: To get an approximate value so subsequent careful titrations know when endpoint is near
questions.answer: To get an approximate value so subsequent careful titrations know when endpoint is near
questions.explanation: A rough titre gives an approximate endpoint. Accurate subsequent titrations can then be added dropwise near the endpoint.

questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Stoichiometry. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Stoichiometry concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of chemistry.
Core Theory

This section of Stoichiometry builds on foundational knowledge. In chemistry, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 4.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Stoichiometry. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 4.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Stoichiometry. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Stoichiometry include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Stoichiometry. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).

questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).
